Meta.title: Lidless — Keep your Mac awake while it's locked
Meta.description: Lidless is a tiny macOS menu bar app that stops your Mac from sleeping while the screen is locked, so long tasks, downloads and remote sessions keep running. Let the display sleep; keep the system awake.
Meta.ogAlt: Lidless — keep your Mac awake while locked

Hero.titleLine2: Keep the system awake. Let the display sleep.
Hero.subtitle: Your Mac looks asleep — but downloads, builds and remote sessions keep running behind a locked, dark screen. The display sleeps to save power; the system stays awake. One switch, that's it.
Hero.trust: No account. No daemon. No dock icon.

AppPanel.title: Lidless
AppPanel.tag: Stay awake when locked · stay online
AppPanel.footnote: Closing the window = minimize to tray

Features.title: Small app, one job, done right
Features.subtitle: No bloat, no background services you can't see. Just the controls that matter.

items.title: One-tap keep awake
items.description: Flip one switch and your Mac won't fall asleep. The screen still dims and locks normally — only idle system sleep is blocked.

items.title: Only when plugged in
items.description: Optionally pause on battery and resume the moment you plug back in. Great for laptops you don't want draining on the couch.

items.title: Lives in the menu bar
items.description: No dock icon, no always-on window. Click the tray icon for a little panel; click away and it tucks itself back while staying active.

items.title: Cross-platform
items.description: The same one-switch tool runs on Windows too, with a Modern Standby hint when your wireless adapter would otherwise nap.

items.title: Featherweight & native
items.description: Built on Tauri with hand-written system calls — no Electron, no heavyweight runtime. It just sits there sipping nothing.

items.title: Nothing leaves your Mac
items.description: No account, no telemetry, no network calls of its own. Lidless only talks to the OS power APIs — and nothing else.

UseCases.title: Built for the moments you step away
UseCases.subtitle: Lock the screen, walk off, and let the machine keep working. A few of the things people leave running:

How.title: How it actually works
How.subtitle: No tricks, no fake mouse jiggling. Lidless asks macOS — politely, the supported way — to hold off on sleep.

steps.title: It holds a power assertion
steps.description: On macOS, Lidless creates an IOKit PreventUserIdleSystemSleep assertion — the same mechanism the OS itself uses. The system stays awake; the display is free to sleep.

steps.title: It watches the power source
steps.description: A single background thread polls power state. With "only when plugged in" on, unplugging cleanly releases the assertion and plugging back in restores it.

steps.title: It gets out of your way
steps.description: No persistent window, no dock clutter. The tray panel appears on click and hides on blur, while the assertion keeps holding in the background.

How.techNote: All OS calls are hand-written FFI (IOKit on macOS, SetThreadExecutionState on Windows) with no third-party power crates. Open source — read every line on GitHub.

Faq.title: Frequently asked questions
Faq.subtitle: The things people ask before downloading.

items.q: Does the screen stay on the whole time?
items.a: No — and that's the point. Your display sleeps and locks normally to save power and stay private. Only the system's idle sleep is blocked, so background work keeps running behind a locked, dark screen.

items.q: How is this different from Amphetamine or Caffeine?
items.a: Same core idea — keep the Mac awake — but Lidless is deliberately minimal: one switch, a plugged-in-only option, and a tray panel that disappears when you don't need it. It's also cross-platform and fully open source.

items.q: How is it different from the caffeinate command?
items.a: caffeinate -i is the built-in macOS terminal command that holds the same kind of power assertion — but it stops the moment you close the terminal window, and there's no UI or plugged-in-only option. Lidless is a persistent menu bar GUI for that exact mechanism: flip it once and it keeps holding in the background. You can confirm it's working any time with pmset -g assertions.

items.q: Will it keep my Mac online for remote access?
items.a: Yes. Because the system never goes to sleep, network stays up, so SSH, RustDesk, VNC and Screen Sharing remain reachable while the Mac is locked.
Keeping the system awake uses more power than sleeping, so on a laptop we recommend the "only when plugged in" option — Lidless then pauses on battery and resumes when you plug in.

items.q: Is it safe? What does it access?
items.a: Lidless only calls the operating system's power-management APIs. No account, no telemetry, no network traffic of its own. The full source is on GitHub if you'd like to verify.

Yes — use the Download for Windows button above (or grab the .msi from the GitHub releases page). The same one-switch app runs on Windows, with an extra hint for Modern Standby machines whose Wi-Fi adapter would otherwise sleep.

CtaBand.title: Keep the work awake. Walk away.
CtaBand.subtitle: Free, open source, and out of your way.
